name: Figure 02
short_description: A general-purpose humanoid robot aimed at manipulation and labor tasks in human environments.
long_description: A bipedal humanoid with arms, hands, and onboard cameras intended to work in warehouses and other human-shaped spaces. Its anthropomorphic form sets strong expectations of dexterity and instruction-following, which is exactly the expectation-mismatch risk humanoids carry.

images.caption: Figure 02 humanoid robot.
images.alt_text: The torso and arm of the Figure 02 humanoid robot, a grey and black machine with a glossy black faceplate, looking down at its own hand.

interactions.title: Load sheet metal onto the welding line
interactions.description: On the floor at BMW Spartanburg, the robot picks sheet-metal parts from a rack and seats them on a welding fixture within a 5 mm tolerance, working 10-hour shifts that helped build over 30,000 X3 vehicles.

interactions.title: Put the groceries away when simply asked
interactions.description: Tell the pair of robots "can you put these away?" and they survey the counter, divide up unfamiliar items, open the fridge and drawers, and stow each thing — then close the drawer and shut the fridge without being told.

interactions.title: Hand the cookies to the robot on your right
interactions.description: Two Figure 02 units running identical Helix weights coordinate purely from plain-language prompts — "hand the bag of cookies to the robot on your right" — passing objects between them with no pre-programmed choreography or assigned roles.

interactions.title: Pick up 'the desert item'
interactions.description: Asked for an abstract category rather than a named object, the robot reasons over the scene and plucks a toy cactus from a pile of unfamiliar things — grounding language in vision without a per-object demonstration.

interactions.title: Pat a wrinkled poly bag flat to read its barcode
interactions.description: Sorting packages for an hour straight, the robot meets a soft mailer whose label lies on a curved surface and briefly presses and smooths it flat so the scanner can see the barcode — a tactic that emerged from training data, never explicitly programmed.

interactions.title: The uncanny pause where two robots 'stare' at each other
interactions.description: Watching the kitchen demo, observers noticed the two units freeze and seem to look at one another between handoffs — a telepathic-looking beat that reads as deliberation but is really the shared policy resolving who acts next.

interactions.title: Alpha-test life in a real, messy home
interactions.description: Figure plans early in-home testing where the same body that loaded car panels must instead navigate uneven lighting, scattered clutter, pets, and stairs while doing chores like food prep — a far harder, less structured world than the factory.

interactions.title: Voice-direct a tote-to-shelf restock
interactions.description: A worker could speak a stocking goal — "empty this tote onto the second shelf, fragile items at the front" — and let the robot interpret and execute it, building on the same language-grounded pick-and-place and barcode handling it already does in logistics demos.
